title: SEGMENT 9 — Cell wall, continued: vancomycin and the others

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Cell wall, continued: vancomycin and the others

Beta-lactams aren't the only cell-wall-active drugs, and the most important of the others is vancomycin — a member of the glycopeptide class, and a drug you'll hear about constantly because it's a workhorse against resistant gram-positives, including MRSA.

Here's what's clever about vancomycin, and why I'm covering it right after beta-lactams: it attacks the same construction project — peptidoglycan cross-linking — but at a completely different point, using a completely different mechanism.
The beta-lactams bind the enzyme, the transpeptidase.
Vancomycin ignores the enzyme entirely.
Instead, vancomycin binds the substrate — it grabs that D-Ala-D-Ala terminus directly, the very tail end of the peptide chain, before the enzyme can get to it.
Vancomycin is a big, bulky molecule, and once it caps the D-Ala-D-Ala, it physically gets in the way.
The transpeptidase can't access its substrate because there's a huge drug molecule sitting on it, and even the sugar-chain-extending step is blocked.
So cross-linking fails, the wall can't be assembled, and again you get a fatally weakened cell wall.
Bactericidal, same end result, but by steric obstruction of the substrate rather than inactivation of the enzyme.
Now here's a consequence of vancomycin's size that I want you to reason through, because it illustrates a principle.
Vancomycin is a large molecule.
Gram-negative bacteria have that outer membrane I keep flagging, studded with narrow channels called porins, and large molecules simply can't get through.
So vancomycin is essentially useless against gram-negatives — not because their transpeptidase is different, but because the drug physically can't reach it.
It's locked out at the front door.
Gram-positives have no such outer membrane, so vancomycin reaches their wall freely.
This is your first clean example of a recurring theme: sometimes a drug fails against an organism not because the target is wrong, but because the drug can't physically get to the target.
Access is half the battle.
And how do bacteria resist vancomycin?
Think about it mechanistically — the drug binds D-Ala-D-Ala, so the resistance is to change the target it binds.
Vancomycin-resistant organisms swap that terminal D-alanine for a D-lactate: D-Ala-D-Lac instead of D-Ala-D-Ala.
It's a tiny chemical change — one atom swapped, an oxygen for an NH — but it dramatically weakens vancomycin's grip while still allowing cross-linking to proceed.
That single substitution drops vancomycin's binding affinity roughly a thousand-fold.
That's the basis of VRE, vancomycin-resistant enterococci.
Notice the theme repeating: target modification as a resistance strategy, just like PBP2a for the beta-lactams.
Same logic, different molecule.
Let me round out the cell-wall category briefly with a few more agents, because they show the wall can be attacked at every stage of its construction.
Fosfomycin blocks one of the earliest steps — the synthesis of the muramic acid building block, before the sugar chain is even assembled.

Bacitracin blocks the recycling of the lipid carrier molecule that ferries the building blocks across the membrane to the construction site — knock out the delivery truck and the materials never arrive; bacitracin's mostly topical because it's too toxic for systemic use.
And cycloserine, used in tuberculosis, blocks the enzymes that make the D-Ala-D-Ala unit in the first place.
So you can see the whole picture: early synthesis, transport, assembly, and final cross-linking — and we have a drug that hits essentially every step.
The cell wall is such a good target, with such a big selective-toxicity advantage, that medicinal chemistry has attacked it from every conceivable angle.
That's target category one, the cell wall — the biggest difference between bacteria and us, and home to our safest and most beloved drugs.
Let's take a break here.

We've done the cell wall and all the pharmacodynamic framing; when we come back, we'll do the ribosome, which is a richer and more crowded target, and then nucleic acids, folate, the membrane, and finally we'll pull the whole resistance story together.
Stretch, and I'll see you in a few minutes.
